0: <title>Impact of smallholder cattle farms on the environment: a study </title>
Rakesh Kumar Gautam Singh2 Ekta Rani
Received: 2 March 2023 / Accepted: 6 June 2023 / Published online: 12 June 2023 © The Author(s), under exclusive licence to Springer Nature B.V. 2023
1: With increasing evidence of global warming, the pressure is building to limit greenhouse gas emission from many human activities including dairy production systems. In this context, the present study was conducted to estimate the carbon footprint (CF) of cattle milk produced in the Hisar district of Haryana, India. The data about feeding practices, crops grown, manure management systems, etc. was collected through personal interviews with cattle-rearing rural male farmers chosen through multistep random sampling. The life cycle assessment (LCA) methodology was adopted to estimate carbon footprint, with the system boundary being "Cradle to farm gate." The latest methodologies prescribed by the IPCC were used to estimate GHG emissions using the tier-2 approach. The current study has presented a detailed and recent GHG inventory from smallholder cattle farms at the village level. On the basis of the inventory analysis, a simplified life cycle-based analysis is used in order to quantify the carbon footprint of fat- and protein-corrected milk (FPCM). The carbon footprint of cattle milk was estimated at 2.13 kg CO2-eq/kilogram FPCM. Enteric fermentation was the most potent contributor to GHG, contributing around 35.5% of the total emissions, followed by manure management (13.8%) and soil management (8.2%). Further studies to accurately estimate carbon footprint are advocated besides suggesting ways to reduce GHG emissions and using efficient production technologies.
Keywords Carbon footprint (CF) . Greenhouse gas (GHG) . Life cycle assessment (LCA) . Cattle

3: One of the most significant human-caused contributors to greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions is the livestock industry (Dhoubhadel et al., 2016). Emissions from "Agriculture, Forestry, and Other Land Use" have almost quadrupled over the previous 50 years, and they are expected to rise much higher by 2050 (Tubiello et al., 2014). Livestock accounts for the vast majority (64-78%) of the livestock industry's total emissions (Herrero et al., 2016). A grow- ing global population, more dairy consumption (Baek et al., 2014; Adler et al., 2015), and other factors all contribute to a yearly growth in greenhouse gas emissions linked with dairy products. GHG emissions will rise as the global population
grows and people consume more dairy and meat products, but not if production efficiency and management are dra- matically improved to counteract these trends (Rowlinson et al., 2008).

Approximately 20.5% of India's population relies on agriculture for their income, and the livestock system is a crucial element of it. It accounts for 4.4% of India's GDP in 2007-2008 and contributes significantly to the social and economic development of millions of rural families (Kumar and Singh, 2008; Patra, 2012). The total livestock popula- tion and number of cattle were 535.78 and 192.49 million, respectively, in the country in 2019 (Anonymous 2019). On the flip side, with this huge population of cattle and poorly managed production system, it can have a miscellany of detrimental impacts on the environment, including water scarcity, water pollution, nutrient accumulation, overgraz- ing, land degradation, and greenhouse gas emissions (Owen et al., 2005). Additionally, India is the third-greatest contrib- utor to global greenhouse gas emissions, accounting for 7% of total emissions (Pahuja et al., 2014). It has been estimated that the livestock sector emitted 14.53 Mt CH4 and 0.178 Mt N2O, and the total GHG emissions in terms of CO2-eq were 243.128 Mt for the year 2015 (Dhingra et al., 2019).

Efficient emission reduction targets need an assessment of greenhouse gas emissions from the livestock sector (Shilpi et al., 2018). Carbon footprint, defined as the sum of a prod- uct's or service's carbon emissions across its full life cycle, is a useful measure for demonstrating the low-carbon idea (Vergé et al., 2013; Dong et al., 2014). The present study made use of the LCA approach to estimate the carbon foot- print of milk in terms of CO2-eq per unit of FPCM using the IPCC (2019) Guidelines for National Greenhouse Gas Inventories, with the tier-2 approach. The tier-2 characteri- zation is better, as it requires detailed information to define animals, animal productivity, diet quality, and management circumstances to support a more accurate estimate of feed intake for use in estimating GHG emission.

Almost no studies of CF have been conducted in rural Indian farming communities. Due to the lack of data, we decided to measure the CF of milk produced by cows on Indian smallholder farms. In light of these considerations, the article provides a summary of research undertaken in the Hisar area of Haryana State, India.

Almost no studies of CF have been conducted in rural Indian farming communities. Due to the lack of data, we decided to measure the CF of milk produced by cows on Indian smallholder farms. In light of these considerations, the article provides a summary of research undertaken in the Hisar area of Haryana State, India.
6: This research was performed in the Hisar area of Haryana, India (Fig. 1), which is classified as a hot arid eco-sub-region lying in the transgangetic plain region (western agroclimatic

zone). Farms were selected at random from the Hisar area's mixed crop-livestock system, and all were owned by small- holders. Field data was gathered in a methodical manner. The data was collected from the cattle-rearing farmers using direct interviews and observation over 1 year. A sample size of 115 cattle was used in the present study.
7: Various indicators and tools have been developed for assess- ing and standardizing the environmental impacts of differ- ent imbalances in the system. Among them is the widely established process of conducting a product's environmen- tal impact assessment throughout the course of its full life cycle, known as a life cycle assessment (LCA) (Guinée and Lindeijer 2002). The LCA technique is well suited for assessing agricultural systems since it offers an impartial means of characterizing the production system and evaluat- ing its effects in terms of outputs (Casey and Holden, 2005). The International Organization for Standardization's (ISO) ISO 14040 (ISO, 2006a) and ISO 14044 LCA (ISO, 2006b) criteria were followed throughout this investigation.

9: The accuracy of the evaluation depends critically on the sys- tem boundary, a major part of life cycle assessment (LCA) (Park et al., 2016). "Cradle to farm gate" boundary has been utilized in the study, i.e., the process of milk production starts at the creation of the farm inputs and finishes at the farm gate (milk production). Figure 2 represents the system boundary. One area of concern in LCA is the allocation. The allocation usually allows to show the environmental bur- den associated with the process with multiple co-products. However, ISO 14044 (2006b) recommends that whenever possible, allocation should be avoided. In the present study, no allocation strategy was also adopted. 10: During the interview, detailed information about the house- hold, agriculture farm size, herd composition of cattle farms, types and quantity of fodder cultivation for cattle, feeding and management, milk production, and manure management were collected. Animals' body weights were determined using Shaeffer's method, based on the measurements of their heart girths taken on field trips.

11: The quantification of the CF was done by using the data col- lected. All the GHG emissions from various sources were estimated, converted into kilogram CO2-eq figures, and then added together to arrive at the CF of the cattle milk (expressed in CO2-eq). The study utilized tier-2 emission factors, adapting from combinations of guidelines and data- bases, which are as follows:

<list>(a) IPCC-2019, Guidelines for National Greenhouse Gas Inventories. 
(b) Emission factor database of the IPCC, 2019. 
(c) Emission factors for feed ingredients were taken from Ecoinvent v.2 (Ecoinvent, 2010). 
(d) Emission factors for fuel combustion and seed produc- tion were taken from APAT (2003) and Pirlo and Care (2013), respectively.</list>

1: Estimation is based on data on lactating cattle from village- level cattle farms in the Hisar district of Haryana using an appropriate sampling procedure. Table 1 summarizes the most important aspects of homes and farms. Male respond- ents were chosen deliberately because a male member of the family is known to take decisions regarding animal hus- bandry in the study area (Anika, 2014).

Concentrates, agricultural leftovers, and a little quantity of green grass were all that the animals in the stalls had to eat. Animals were given seasonal variations of green forage. Cows who were breastfeeding were the primary recipients of the more expensive foods like concentrates. The average quantities of feed and fodder on a dry matter (DM) basis fed to lactating cattle per day were 11.22 kg day (Table 2). Similar results for dry matter intake have earlier been reported by Dixit and Birthal (2016). The average digest- ibility of the ration was taken as 55% as per the IPCC (2019).

These range from 28 + 5 kg CH4 head-1 year (NATCOM, 2004) to 74 kg CH4 head year (IPCC, 2019) for Indian cattle. However, a majority of these stud- ies have relied on tier-1, whereas the present study utilized the tier-2 approach of the IPCC (2019) to estimate methane emission factors.

Also, the variations in the estimates from the IPCC assessment, at least in part, can be attributed to the lower average body weight used by the IPCC, i.e., 275 kg, which is significantly lower than the estimated average body weight and use of the tier-1 methodology, which relies on default emission factors drawn from the literature. The estimated mean body weight of lactating was 387.6 kg. Thus, the dif- ferences are easily understandable.

Almost half of the dung from cattle farms was used for making dung cakes. The remaining half was stored as a dry lot that was later used as fertilizer for the crops. The farm- ers, in general, store dry dung cake for generally 1 year. It has been earlier reported that around 2% of methane emis- sion occur during the course of the storage of dry dung and 10% of methane emission occur during the burning of those dry dung (IPCC 2019). Estimated methane emissions from manure management were found to be 17.3 kg CH4 head-¹ year in the current investigation. Similar findings were published by Pirlo and Care (2013) of 15.04 kg CH4 head-¹ year emission from dairy cow dung. This is much greater than the 3.8 kg CH4 head year emission recorded before (Jha et al., 2011). Methane emissions from manure man- agement account for 6.97% of total CO2-eq emission in the current research. A previous study by Chhabra et al. In the dairy industry, methane emis- sions from manure management typically contribute 8-19% of total CO2-eq emissions (Geough et al., 2012; Phetteplace et al., 2001; Rotz et al., 2010; Schils et al., 2005). The dif- ference between estimates of the present study and those of other researchers for the manure methane emission factor may be attributed to higher dry matter intake in the study area. The ash concentration of dung was estimated to be 17% rather than the 8% used by the IPCC, and this led to a differ- ent emission factor for manure management. This, in turn, relates to gross energy intake and higher volatile solid excre- tion. Also, the study adopted the latest IPCC (2019) stand- ards which may have contributed to the variation. Wheat straw, stovers, paddy straw, and other agro-industrial wastes are the primary sources of nutrition for livestock in India. These wastes have a greater ash content and lower volatile solids (VS) than the usual high-quality meals that are fed to livestock in industrialized nations.

lactating buffalo at smallholder farms in Haryana, India, using the IPCC (2006) guidelines and the tier-1 approach. Animal feces contain a significant quantity of nitrogen, the precise amount of which is proportional to the animal's nitrogen and food consumption. Cattle feces have modest levels of quickly decomposable nitrogen but strong urease activity, making them ideal for nitrous oxide synthesis when they come into contact with urine. Urea is quickly hydrolyzed when it comes into contact with urine (Bussink and Oenema, 1998). The direct and indirect production of nitrous oxide occurs during the treatment and storage of manure. The direct nitrous oxide (N2O was estimated to be higher (85%) than the indirect N2OG (13%) and N2OL (2%) of the total N20 emissions from manure management (Table 3). Nitrous oxide generation is proportional to manure volume, manure type, manure man- agement method, and waste storage time (Jha et al., 2011).

Nitrous oxide emissions from fodder cultivation for cattle farms were computed based on data collected about green fodder inputs for cattle, cultivated crops, land size, and annual production of each crop biomass. The direct nitrous oxide emission was estimated to be higher (80%) than the indirect N2OG (1%) and N2OL (19%) emissions.

Nitrous oxide emission was 15% (manure management and managed soil constitute 6.9% and 8.2%, respectively) of the total CO2-eq for lactating cattle. The contribution of emission from manure management was 15% (6.94% N2O and 6.97% CH4), as summarized in Fig. 3. The emissions of nitrous oxide from dairy cattle were in line with Garg et al. (2016) reported 13.6% contribution of total nitrous oxide emission of the total GHGs. Slight variations in the values of nitrous oxide emission may be due to differences in manure management practices. Notably, the values for nitrogen excretion rates, the average body weight, the total nitrogen intake of animals, the selection of level (tier-1/tier-2/tier-3), and the type of productivity class (high/low) may have also contributed to the variations.

lactating buffalo at smallholder farms in Haryana, India, using the IPCC (2006) guidelines and the tier-1 approach. Animal feces contain a significant quantity of nitrogen, the precise amount of which is proportional to the animal's nitrogen and food consumption. Cattle feces have modest levels of quickly decomposable nitrogen but strong urease activity, making them ideal for nitrous oxide synthesis when they come into contact with urine. Urea is quickly hydrolyzed when it comes into contact with urine (Bussink and Oenema, 1998). The direct and indirect production of nitrous oxide occurs during the treatment and storage of manure. The direct nitrous oxide (N2O was estimated to be higher (85%) than the indirect N2OG (13%) and N2OL (2%) of the total N20 emissions from manure management (Table 3). Nitrous oxide generation is proportional to manure volume, manure type, manure man- agement method, and waste storage time (Jha et al., 2011).

Nitrous oxide emissions from fodder cultivation for cattle farms were computed based on data collected about green fodder inputs for cattle, cultivated crops, land size, and annual production of each crop biomass. The direct nitrous oxide emission was estimated to be higher (80%) than the indirect N2OG (1%) and N2OL (19%) emissions.

Nitrous oxide emission was 15% (manure management and managed soil constitute 6.9% and 8.2%, respectively) of the total CO2-eq for lactating cattle. The contribution of emission from manure management was 15% (6.94% N2O and 6.97% CH4), as summarized in Fig. 3. The emissions of nitrous oxide from dairy cattle were in line with Garg et al. (2016) reported 13.6% contribution of total nitrous oxide emission of the total GHGs. Slight variations in the values of nitrous oxide emission may be due to differences in manure management practices. Notably, the values for nitrogen excretion rates, the average body weight, the total nitrogen intake of animals, the selection of level (tier-1/tier-2/tier-3), and the type of productivity class (high/low) may have also contributed to the variations.

12: The highest emissions in this study, directly from lactating cattle, were attributable to enteric fermentation (82%), fol- lowed by CH4 from manure management (16%) and N2O from manure management (2%). Results were in line with FAO (2017), which reported that the maximum direct emis- sion from dairy cattle was through enteric fermentation (85%), followed by CH4 from 8% of manure management along with N2O from 7% of manure management. Table 4 displays the annual CO2-equivalent emissions of greenhouse gases for lactating cattle from various emission sources throughout the supply chain. The supply chain's main source of emissions has been the production of feed (50.5%), fol- lowed by enteric fermentation (35.5%), and manure manage- ment (13.9%), as shown in Fig. 3. Gerber et al. (2013) found comparable figures, with 45% of the total GHG emissions in the cattle supply chain coming from feed production, 39% from enteric fermentation, and 10% from manure manage- ment. Dry fodder has the highest CO2-eq emission among all feed production sources. The carbon footprint for lactat- ing cattle using the IPCC 100-year characterizations was estimated at 2.13 kg CO2-eq/kilogram FPCM. One kilogram of FPCM produces 0.726 kg CO2-eq from enteric fermenta- tion, which constitutes the maximum (35.58%) of the total CO2-eq along the supply chain. The results were in line with Garg et al. (2016), who earlier reported the carbon footprint of cow milk as 2.3 kg CO2-eq/kilogram FPCM for small- holder farmers using the IPCC (2006) guidelines.

Carbon footprint analysis may play a significant part in the progress of livestock production and sustainable agricul- ture. Most of the greenhouse gas (GHG) balance in livestock production systems still has significant open questions. Car- bon sequestration is a crucial but frequently overlooked part of the greenhouse gas (GHG) budget on farms. The dairy sector must find a way to reduce environmental impacts, including global warming potential (GWP). As long as the

current system is modified to make use of mitigation strate- gies that have been shown to be effective in scientific stud- ies, there is hope for improvement. It will need a significant worldwide cooperative effort to reduce emissions and adjust livestock production systems to climate change. Interven- tions like educating farmers and appealing to morals seem best for now. The development of GHG emissions account- ability and abatement tools (e.g., software) can be tried at the commercial level. Small-scale farming is vital to the eco- nomic and nutritional stability of rural communities. Until mitigation solutions provide a higher-earning generation with easy accessibility and cost-effective technology and are linked to incentives demonstrating advantages to their lives, the problem will persist. GHG mitigation and water conservation methods will not be adopted by smallholder farmers because they are insensitive and hard-hearted.

Finally, it is suggested that bringing about productivity enhancement in dairy animals should be the policy goal. But it should be ensured that this is not done at the cost of dis- turbing rural production systems, and other environmental aspects should be taken into consideration. It is agreed that simple ways out are difficult to find, and there is a need for a wider environmental impact assessment before arriving at final solutions.

16: <header>Conclusion </header>

The impact that milk production has on the surrounding eco- system is analyzed in this particular research study. On the basis of the inventory analysis, a simplified life cycle-based analysis is used in order to quantify the carbon footprint of pure milk. This evaluation is based on the life cycle. Accord- ing to the findings, the carbon footprint of milk is 2.13 kg CO2-eq/kilogram FPCM, with enteric fermentation account- ing for the greatest portion of the total contribution at 0.72 kg CO2-eq/kilogram FPCM. The results indicated that a wide

variation among animals exists for GHG emission at similar feed intake and production levels. Therefore, cattle breeding program may consider emission and productivity levels to select efficient animals for improvement of productivity with a lower level of methane emission. Removing the low-produc- ing COWS from the herd and retaining the efficient ones will reduce methane emissions per unit of output. Village-level farms may be able to contribute significantly to greenhouse gas (GHG) reduction if management is improved, proportions of animal feed are changed, and more sustainable consump- tion practices are pursued. There is increasing demand to restrict greenhouse gas emissions from a variety of human activities, including the manufacturing of dairy products, as more and more evidence of global warming emerges. The results of this study might pave the way for future research that investigates the impact of multifunctionality on the CF of milk production in mixed agricultural systems all over the globe. 17: <header>Conclusion </header>

The impact that milk production has on the surrounding eco- system is analyzed in this particular research study. On the basis of the inventory analysis, a simplified life cycle-based analysis is used in order to quantify the carbon footprint of pure milk. This evaluation is based on the life cycle. Accord- ing to the findings, the carbon footprint of milk is 2.13 kg CO2-eq/kilogram FPCM, with enteric fermentation account- ing for the greatest portion of the total contribution at 0.72 kg CO2-eq/kilogram FPCM. The results indicated that a wide

variation among animals exists for GHG emission at similar feed intake and production levels. Therefore, cattle breeding program may consider emission and productivity levels to select efficient animals for improvement of productivity with a lower level of methane emission. Removing the low-produc- ing COWS from the herd and retaining the efficient ones will reduce methane emissions per unit of output. Village-level farms may be able to contribute significantly to greenhouse gas (GHG) reduction if management is improved, proportions of animal feed are changed, and more sustainable consump- tion practices are pursued. 18: <header>Conclusion </header>

The impact that milk production has on the surrounding eco- system is analyzed in this particular research study. On the basis of the inventory analysis, a simplified life cycle-based analysis is used in order to quantify the carbon footprint of pure milk. This evaluation is based on the life cycle. Accord- ing to the findings, the carbon footprint of milk is 2.13 kg CO2-eq/kilogram FPCM, with enteric fermentation account- ing for the greatest portion of the total contribution at 0.72 kg CO2-eq/kilogram FPCM. The results indicated that a wide

variation among animals exists for GHG emission at similar feed intake and production levels. Therefore, cattle breeding program may consider emission and productivity levels to select efficient animals for improvement of productivity with a lower level of methane emission. Removing the low-produc- ing COWS from the herd and retaining the efficient ones will reduce methane emissions per unit of output. Village-level farms may be able to contribute significantly to greenhouse gas (GHG) reduction if management is improved, proportions of animal feed are changed, and more sustainable consump- tion practices are pursued. There is increasing demand to restrict greenhouse gas emissions from a variety of human activities, including the manufacturing of dairy products, as more and more evidence of global warming emerges. The results of this study might pave the way for future research that investigates the impact of multifunctionality on the CF of milk production in mixed agricultural systems all over the globe. 20: <header>Conclusion </header>

The impact that milk production has on the surrounding eco- system is analyzed in this particular research study. On the basis of the inventory analysis, a simplified life cycle-based analysis is used in order to quantify the carbon footprint of pure milk. This evaluation is based on the life cycle. Accord- ing to the findings, the carbon footprint of milk is 2.13 kg CO2-eq/kilogram FPCM, with enteric fermentation account- ing for the greatest portion of the total contribution at 0.72 kg CO2-eq/kilogram FPCM. The results indicated that a wide

variation among animals exists for GHG emission at similar feed intake and production levels. Therefore, cattle breeding program may consider emission and productivity levels to select efficient animals for improvement of productivity with a lower level of methane emission. Removing the low-produc- ing COWS from the herd and retaining the efficient ones will reduce methane emissions per unit of output. Village-level farms may be able to contribute significantly to greenhouse gas (GHG) reduction if management is improved, proportions of animal feed are changed, and more sustainable consump- tion practices are pursued. There is increasing demand to restrict greenhouse gas emissions from a variety of human activities, including the manufacturing of dairy products, as more and more evidence of global warming emerges. The results of this study might pave the way for future research that investigates the impact of multifunctionality on the CF of milk production in mixed agricultural systems all over the globe. 22: <header>Conclusion </header>

The impact that milk production has on the surrounding eco- system is analyzed in this particular research study. On the basis of the inventory analysis, a simplified life cycle-based analysis is used in order to quantify the carbon footprint of pure milk. This evaluation is based on the life cycle. Accord- ing to the findings, the carbon footprint of milk is 2.13 kg CO2-eq/kilogram FPCM, with enteric fermentation account- ing for the greatest portion of the total contribution at 0.72 kg CO2-eq/kilogram FPCM. The results indicated that a wide

variation among animals exists for GHG emission at similar feed intake and production levels. Therefore, cattle breeding program may consider emission and productivity levels to select efficient animals for improvement of productivity with a lower level of methane emission. Removing the low-produc- ing COWS from the herd and retaining the efficient ones will reduce methane emissions per unit of output. Village-level farms may be able to contribute significantly to greenhouse gas (GHG) reduction if management is improved, proportions of animal feed are changed, and more sustainable consump- tion practices are pursued. There is increasing demand to restrict greenhouse gas emissions from a variety of human activities, including the manufacturing of dairy products, as more and more evidence of global warming emerges. The results of this study might pave the way for future research that investigates the impact of multifunctionality on the CF of milk production in mixed agricultural systems all over the globe. 24: <header>Conclusion </header>

The impact that milk production has on the surrounding eco- system is analyzed in this particular research study. On the basis of the inventory analysis, a simplified life cycle-based analysis is used in order to quantify the carbon footprint of pure milk. This evaluation is based on the life cycle. Accord- ing to the findings, the carbon footprint of milk is 2.13 kg CO2-eq/kilogram FPCM, with enteric fermentation account- ing for the greatest portion of the total contribution at 0.72 kg CO2-eq/kilogram FPCM. The results indicated that a wide

variation among animals exists for GHG emission at similar feed intake and production levels. Therefore, cattle breeding program may consider emission and productivity levels to select efficient animals for improvement of productivity with a lower level of methane emission. Removing the low-produc- ing COWS from the herd and retaining the efficient ones will reduce methane emissions per unit of output. Village-level farms may be able to contribute significantly to greenhouse gas (GHG) reduction if management is improved, proportions of animal feed are changed, and more sustainable consump- tion practices are pursued. There is increasing demand to restrict greenhouse gas emissions from a variety of human activities, including the manufacturing of dairy products, as more and more evidence of global warming emerges. The results of this study might pave the way for future research that investigates the impact of multifunctionality on the CF of milk production in mixed agricultural systems all over the globe. 26: <header>Conclusion </header>

The impact that milk production has on the surrounding eco- system is analyzed in this particular research study. On the basis of the inventory analysis, a simplified life cycle-based analysis is used in order to quantify the carbon footprint of pure milk. This evaluation is based on the life cycle. Accord- ing to the findings, the carbon footprint of milk is 2.13 kg CO2-eq/kilogram FPCM, with enteric fermentation account- ing for the greatest portion of the total contribution at 0.72 kg CO2-eq/kilogram FPCM. The results indicated that a wide

variation among animals exists for GHG emission at similar feed intake and production levels. Therefore, cattle breeding program may consider emission and productivity levels to select efficient animals for improvement of productivity with a lower level of methane emission. Removing the low-produc- ing COWS from the herd and retaining the efficient ones will reduce methane emissions per unit of output. Village-level farms may be able to contribute significantly to greenhouse gas (GHG) reduction if management is improved, proportions of animal feed are changed, and more sustainable consump- tion practices are pursued. There is increasing demand to restrict greenhouse gas emissions from a variety of human activities, including the manufacturing of dairy products, as more and more evidence of global warming emerges. The results of this study might pave the way for future research that investigates the impact of multifunctionality on the CF of milk production in mixed agricultural systems all over the globe.
